title: Wet-felted pincushion set
subtitle: Three small firm pincushions in natural cream, soft grey, and dusty rose, each around 5 cm across
excerpt: Wet-felt three compact pincushions from merino roving in graduated colours. The method is the same rounded-ball technique for all three; the firmness check confirms the piece is dense enough to hold a pin vertical. A practical sewing-room set that uses leftover roving well.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from the general wet-felting guild tradition. The felted-ball method is standard workshop practice documented widely in guild newsletters and observation-led felting instruction.

glossaryTerms.definition: Wool fibre drawn into a long ribbon ready for spinning or felting. The staples lie roughly parallel along the ribbon's length, making it easy to pull thin layers from the end.
The stage of wet felting that follows initial bonding: the pre-felt is rinsed, then agitated more vigorously to compact the fibres fully and shrink the piece to its finished size.

Wool that has been wet down and worked lightly enough that the fibres have begun to lock but not yet fully compressed. At pre-felt stage the piece holds together but is still spongy; continued fulling compresses it further.

content.text: Three small pincushions in graduated colours make a practical and neat addition to the sewing room. Each is wet-felted from merino
roving

by wrapping a loose wool core and working it into a firm sphere. The finished diameter of each pincushion is around 5 cm. The key measure of a well-made pincushion is density: push a dressmaking pin into the surface and release it; if the pin stays upright, the felt is dense enough. A spongy pincushion lets pins lean and tip over, which defeats the purpose. The three colourways here are natural cream, soft grey, and dusty rose, using undyed and lightly dyed merino respectively.
What you need

items.name: Olive-oil soap bar or mild wool wash
Hot water (as hot as your hands can bear)
Cotton tea towel or tray liner

content.text: Method
Make all three pincushions in sequence using the same method. The instructions below describe one pincushion; repeat for each colour.
Step 1: Build the wool core

Weigh out 15 g of roving for one pincushion. Pull a long wisp from the roving and begin wrapping it into a loose ball about 7 to 8 cm across. Wind in multiple directions, crisscrossing, to avoid a layered structure that will peel apart later. The ball will shrink by roughly 30 per cent during felting, so the starting size of 7 to 8 cm brings the finished piece to around 5 cm. Keep the winding loose; a tight wrapped core traps air bubbles and felts unevenly.
Step 2: Wet down the ball

Mix a small amount of olive-oil soap into hot water in a bowl to make a light suds. Submerge the wool ball and press down gently to saturate it. Cup the ball in both hands and press gently without squeezing hard; the wool needs to be thoroughly wet but not yet compressed. The water should be as hot as your hands can comfortably bear. Cool water slows felting significantly.
Step 3: Work the surface gently

Hold the wet ball in cupped palms and rotate it, applying even light pressure from all directions. Do not squeeze hard at this stage; you are encouraging the surface fibres to begin locking together while maintaining the spherical shape. Work for 3 to 4 minutes, rotating and pressing gently. The ball will feel slimy from the soap; that is correct. Add a few drops of hot water if the surface starts to cool. After 4 minutes the surface should feel slightly more cohesive and the fibres should not pull away freely.
Step 4: Increase pressure and full to density

Once the surface holds, increase hand pressure and begin rolling the ball more firmly between both palms. Roll it on the tea towel with moderate pressure for 3 to 4 minutes, then return to rolling between palms. Alternate between rolling on the cloth and rolling between palms every few minutes. Periodically pick up the ball and throw it firmly onto the tea-towel surface 5 to 6 times to shock the fibres. The ball will shrink noticeably during
fulling
; continue until it reaches approximately 5 cm in diameter and feels firm throughout, not spongy. This typically takes 20 to 25 minutes of active working. At the
pre-felt
stage the ball holds its shape but yields when pressed; the finished pincushion should spring back firmly.
Step 5: The firmness check

Before rinsing, do the firmness check. Push a standard dressmaking pin straight down into the top of the pincushion and release it. If the pin stands upright, the felt is dense enough. If it leans or tips over, continue working for another 5 minutes and test again. A properly fulled pincushion will grip a pin firmly enough that it takes a deliberate push to insert and a deliberate pull to remove.
Step 6: Rinse and dry

Rinse the pincushion thoroughly in hot water to remove all soap, then plunge it into cold water for 20 to 30 seconds. The cold shock tightens the fibres and firms the surface further. Press the pincushion firmly between both palms to extract water; do not wring. Set on a dry towel in a warm spot and leave to dry fully, turning it occasionally so all sides dry evenly. Drying takes 12 to 24 hours depending on the ambient temperature.
Common mistakes

items.problem: The pincushion is spongy after drying and does not hold pins vertical
items.cause: The ball was not fulled for long enough, or the water was not hot enough during working
items.fix: Re-wet the ball in hot soapy water and continue fulling, alternating between palm-rolling and throwing on the cloth. Check after every 5 minutes with the pin test. Hotter water and firm even pressure are both required for a dense result.

items.problem: The wool ball splits or develops a seam line during fulling
items.cause: The original core was wound in parallel layers rather than in crossed directions
items.fix: Wind the new core in multiple cross-hatch directions from the start, not in parallel windings. Once a seam develops it is very difficult to eliminate; it is usually easier to start again with a fresh core.

items.problem: The finished pincushion is not round
items.cause: Uneven pressure during rolling, or rolling only on one flat surface rather than rotating
items.fix: Roll the ball between both palms and consciously rotate your hands to present a fresh face of the ball every few passes. Rolling only on a flat surface flattens one side.

items.problem: The surface fibres are loose and fuzzy after drying
items.cause: The surface was not worked long enough, or the hot-cold rinse shock was skipped
items.fix: The cold-water plunge is important for closing the surface. If the surface is still loose, re-wet in hot soapy water, work the surface with firm pressure for a few minutes, then do a proper cold rinse.

content.text: Care and finishing

A felted pincushion does not need washing under normal use. If the surface becomes dusty or marked, spot-clean with a damp cloth. Full immersion will shrink the piece further and change the shape; avoid unless necessary. Store the pincushion in a dry place away from direct sunlight; prolonged UV exposure will fade dyed roving colours over time. If the surface develops pilling from pin insertion, trim the surface fuzz with sharp embroidery scissors held flat.
